coredata.dc:title: Facile one-pot synthesis of tannic acid-stabilized fluorescent copper nanoclusters and its application as sensing probes for chlortetracycline based on inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yu-yu
Hu, Yu-rui
Chen, Shao-ke
Guo, Ming-zhu
Zhang, Yi-ting
Han, Xin-rui
Zhang, Shen
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a simple and one-pot synthetic strategy was established to prepare copper nanoclusters by using tannic acid as the template and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent. The tannic acid-capped copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs@TA) displayed excitation and emission peaks around 365 and 435 nm with fluorescence quantum yield of 9.8%. Based on the inner filter effect and static quenching mechanisms, the Cu NCs@TA was used to selectively and sensitively determine the chlortetracycline. Under the optimal detection conditions, the excellent linear range of 0.5–200 μM was obtained and the limit of detection (LOD) was gained as 0.084 μM. The satisfactory recoveries of 94.4~103.1% in real bovine serum samples indicated that this platform could be employed in practical test of chlortetracycline.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Tannic acid
Fluorescence determination
Chlortetracycline

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV–vis absorption of Cu NCs@TA (black line) and TA (red line), the fluorescent excitation (blue line) and emission (green line) spectra of Cu NCs@TA; (B) The TEM image of Cu NCs@TA; (C) The XPS spectrum of Cu in Cu NCs@TA; (D) The FTIR spectrum of Cu NCs@TA.
(A) The effect of pH (A), reaction time (B), reaction temperature (C) and NaCl concentration (D) on the Cu NCs@TA for CTC detection.

(A) F0/F of the detection system in the different controls and CTC (From left to right: Blank, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, Pro, Gly, Cys, Glu, Ala, PEN, AMP, STR, MY, CHL, CN, CTC, GSH, Fe3+); (B) The photograph of detection system in the presence of different controls and CTC under UV lamp.

The fluorescence intensity of detection system with different CTC concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 130, 140, 150, 160, 170, 180, 190 and 200 μM) (inset: the linear curve between ln(F0/F) and concentration of CTC; the photograph of detection system in the presence of different CTC concentration under UV lamp).
Abridged general view of the as-synthesized Cu NCs@TA and detection for CTC.
Comparison of the different methods for CTC sensing.
Parameters used to calculate IFE of CTC and Cu NCs@TA.
The CTC recoveries in bovine serum samples.

head.ce:title: Facile one-pot synthesis of tannic acid-stabilized fluorescent copper nanoclusters and its application as sensing probes for chlortetracycline based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Arts, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Arts, Taiyuan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Arts, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a simple and one-pot synthetic strategy was established to prepare copper nanoclusters by using tannic acid as the template and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent. The tannic acid-capped copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs@TA) displayed excitation and emission peaks around 365 and 435 nm with fluorescence quantum yield of 9.8%. Based on the inner filter effect and static quenching mechanisms, the Cu NCs@TA was used to selectively and sensitively determine the chlortetracycline. Under the optimal detection conditions, the excellent linear range of 0.5–200 μM was obtained and the limit of detection (LOD) was gained as 0.084 μM. The satisfactory recoveries of 94.4~103.1% in real bovine serum samples indicated that this platform could be employed in practical test of chlortetracycline.

A “turn-off” probe for chlortetracycline detection by tannic acid-stabilized copper nanoclusters.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Chlortetracycline (CTC, Fig. S1), as a highly effective broad-spectrum antibiotic, has a strong inhibitory effect on a variety of pathogens and can be used in the treatment of various infectious diseases. So it is commonly applied as veterinary medicine and feed additive for various animals [1–3]. However, the residues in animal meat, milk, eggs and other foods seriously threaten human health and cause some diseases, such as aplastic anemia and granular leukemia. Furthermore, low concentrations of residues can also induce drug resistance of pathogenic bacteria [4–6]. The European Union and United States have explicitly banned the use of CTC in the production of animal-derived foods and established strict limit standards [7,8]. For example, the maximum residue limits of CTC in muscle, liver and kidney of all edible animals are 0.1, 0.3 and 0.6 mg·kg−1, respectively. Additionally, China has completely banned the addition of antibiotics to animal food since 2020. The severe test of CTC becomes an urgent task under these circumstances.

To date, some analytical means, such as UPLC-UV [9], Raman spectroscopy [10], ELISA [11] and microbial method [12], have been utilized to analyze the concentration of CTC. Although these technical methods display excellent sensitivity and accuracy, the disadvantages of expensive and cumbersome equipment, time-consuming sample pretreatment and method establishment also exist [13–15]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop further more facile, rapid, selective and sensitive technologies for sensing CTC concentration.

Recently, fluorescent methods have gained tremendous interest due to their simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity [16–19]. The core of fluorescent methods is the preparation of fluorescence probe [20,21]. Fluorescent probes mainly include organic dyes [22,23], carbon quantum dots [24,25] and metal nanoclusters (M NCs) [26–28]. For example, Zhang et al. [29] prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots based on the hawthorn for the detection of CTC through inner filter effect. However, harsh preparation conditions limited the wide application of quantum dots [30–32]. Compared with quantum dots, M NCs have received more and more attention because of its unique characteristics, such as water solubility, high fluorescence quantum-yield, notable catalytic behavior, biocompatibility and good stability [33]. Owing to their low toxicity and ultrafine size, M NCs have emerged as promising candidates in the fields of molecular imaging, biosensors, and catalysis [34–36]. Since the size of M NCs is equivalent to the Fermi wavelength of electrons, M NCs have electronic transitions with adjustable sizes and excellent luminescence properties [37,38]. Due to the lower price, copper nanoclusters gradually replace gold and silver nanoclusters.

Metal nanoclusters can be synthesized by electrochemical synthesis method [39], one-pot chemical reduction method [40] and microwave-assisted method [41]. As the important component of nanoclusters, the protective agents increase the stability and solubility through offering a stabilizing layer on the surface of M NCs. Various fluorescent M NCs can be easily prepared by using protecting agents of surfactants [42], DNA[43,44], dendrimers [45,46], thiols [47,48], proteins [49,50] and some other small molecules [51,52]. For example, Wang et al. prepared bovine serum albumin stabilized Cu NCs and sensed hydrogen peroxide in real samples [53]. Bai et al. developed a simple preparation strategy for synthesis of Cu NCs by using folic acid as the protective agent, and the Cu NCs were successfully applied for the detection of sulfadiazine sodium [54]. Chai et al. established DNA-template Cu nanoclusters and the sensor was employed to detect hydrogen peroxide with the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.5 μM (S/N = 3) [55]. Furthermore, tannic acid (TA) which contains a large amount of oxygen-containing functional groups has been used as the protective agent to synthesize Cu NCs. For example, Cang et al. developed a dual detecting sensor for the test of nitrite and cyanide ions based on the TA capped-Cu NCs [56]. Cao et al. prepared an “off-to-on” probe based on TA-Cu NCs which was used to detect Eu3+ ion and phosphate ion [57]. Meanwhile, the TA-Cu NCs was also applied for the sensing of ferric ions and cells imaging [58]. Nevertheless, the literatures of tannic acid-stabilized fluorescent copper nanoclusters as sensors for drugs detecting are rarely reported. Moreover, there are rare reports on the sense of CTC based on Cu NCs.

In this study, TA was chosen as the protective agent to synthesize TA-capped Cu NCs through one-step reduction method. CTC adsorbed on the Cu NCs@TA surface could turn off the fluorescence of Cu NCs@TA. The blue-emitting Cu NCs@TA probes showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity for sensing of CTC, and gained satisfactory recoveries in real samples.

The UV–vis spectra of Cu NCs@TA were obtained from 2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of Cu NCs@TA were carried out on the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) was applied to the analysis of surface functional group for Cu NCs@TA. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) data were recorded on the ESCALAB 250XI (Thermo Fisher Scientific, United States). The FEI Tecnai F30 (Philips-FEI, United States) was used to analyze the element composition and element valence of the Cu NCs@TA under 200 kV. The fluorescence lifetimes of Cu NCs@TA with or without CTC were recorded on the FLS-1000 combined fluorescence lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The fluorescent color of Cu NCs@TA was observed under ZF-1 three-purpose ultraviolet analyzer. The fluorescence pictures of Cu NCs@TA were received by camera. The mixture was stirred through the DF-101SZ magnetic stirrer (Gongyi Kerui Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The FE20 pH-meter (Mettler Toledo Instrument Inc., Shanghai, China) was employed to measure pH values.

All the compounds were directly employed without further purified. Copper chloride (CuCl2, AR), tannic acid (TA, AR) and ascorbic acid (AA, >99.0%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, FeCl3, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), proline (Pro), glutamic acid (Glu), alanine (Ala), ampicillin (AMP), glutathione (GSH), penicillin (PEN), lincomycin hrdrochloride (MY), streptomycin (STR), chloramphenicol (CHL), cephalexin (CN) and chlortetracycline (CTC) were analytically pure and obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Millipore Milli-Q was applied to produce Ultra-pure water (18.25 M·cm−1).

All glassware was soaked in Aqua Regia solution (v(HCl)/v(HNO3)= 3:1) at room temperature for 24 h and simultaneously washed by ethanol and ultrapure water many times. The Cu NCs@TA was synthesized through one-pot reaction [57]. Firstly, CuCl2 solution (200 μL, 0.1 M) and TA solution (100 μL, 0.001 M) were dropped into 20 mL water under stirring. After 5 min of reaction, AA solution (500 μL, 0.4 M) was slowly dropped into the above solution at 50 ℃ for 6 h. The color of the solution varied from colorless to light yellow. At the same time, blue fluorescence could be observed under ZF-1 three-purpose ultraviolet analyzer. The as-developed Cu NCs@TA was purified through dialysis membrane (3000 Da) and stored at 4 ℃ for the future use.

A typical process for measuring of CTC was performed as following: 1 mL as-synthesized Cu NCs@TA and 1 mL PBS buffer (pH=6.0) were mixed thoroughly. Then, various concentrations of CTC were dropped into sensing system. After reaction for 120 s at room temperature, the fluorescent spectra were obtained at the emission wavelength of 435 nm.

To study the fluorescent selectivity of the Cu NCs@TA towards CTC, a series of interferers, including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cl-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, Gly, Cys, Pro, Glu, Ala, AMP, PEN, MY, STR, CHL, GSH and CN, were employed in the selectivity tests.

The bovine serum samples were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China and centrifuged to remove larger particles. Then the bovine serum samples were diluted 50 times by PBS buffer (pH=6.0). Different concentrations of CTC were spiked into the above serum samples. The fluorescent intensities of spiked samples were recorded and the recoveries were obtained through relevant tests and calculations.

The blue fluorescence Cu NCs@TA were developed through a “one-pot” strategy, in which TA and AA were the template agent and reducing reagent ( Scheme 1). The as-prepared Cu NCs@TA was characterized by different techniques. The UV–vis absorption spectra of TA (red line) and Cu NCs@TA (black line) were shown in Fig. 1A. Compared with TA, an absorption peak at 350 nm was found in the spectrum of Cu NCs@TA, suggesting the formation of Cu NCs@TA. At the same time, the colorless solution (TA, illustration a) became light yellow solution (Cu NCs@TA, illustration b) (inset of Fig. 1A). The absence of absorption peak in the range from 500 to 600 nm demonstrated that the nanocluster particle size did not exceed 3.5 nm [59,60]. The fluorescent excitation (blue line) and emission wavelength (green line) of the Cu NCs@TA at 365 and 435 nm were also illustrated in Fig. 1A, respectively. Blue fluorescence could be observed under ZF-1 three-purpose ultraviolet analyzer for Cu NCs@TA solution (illustration d), while TA solution (illustration c) did not have any fluorescent color (inset of Fig. 1A). The quantum yield of Cu NCs@TA was measured as 9.8% by adopting quinine sulfate as the standard detection substance and the value was consistent with the reported literatures [61].

The particle size, morphology and dispersion of Cu NCs@TA were investigated through TEM characterization. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the dispersion of Cu NCs@TA was very well and no aggregation was found. According to statistics, the particle size distribution of Cu NCs@TA was between 1.2 and 2.5 nm, and the average particle size was 2.1 nm.

The XPS was applied to investigate the element composition and element valence of the Cu NCs@TA. As revealed in Fig. S2, the Cu NCs@TA consisted of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and copper elements. Four peaks at 287.5, 399.1, 531.2 and 930.6 eV corresponded to C1s, N1s, O1s and Cu2p, respectively. Meanwhile, the spectrum of Cu appeared two absorption peaks at 931.9 and 952.4 eV, which were assumed to the binding energies of Cu(0) for Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 1C) [62–64]. Subsequently, the FT-IR technique was applied to study the surface groups of the Cu NCs@TA. As displayed in Fig. 1D, the absorption peak at 3432.20 cm−1 originated from O-H band, which were consistent with the reported papers [65,66]. The absorption peak of C-H band was found at 2963.32 cm−1. The transmission peaks at 1642.45 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 suggested the presence of CO and C-O-C bands. The transmission peaks at 1580 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 could be assumed to the benzene ring skeleton vibration. The FTIR results indicated that the surface of Cu NCs@TA has been successfully modified by TA.

The stability of Cu NCs@TA was studied under different conditions. As shown in Figs. S3, 4 ℃ and room temperature were selected as storage temperature for the Cu NCs@TA. After 5 months, the fluorescent intensities of Cu NCs@TA could be maintained 95% and 90% at 4 ℃ and at room temperature, respectively. It was suggested that the Cu NCs@TA intimated a better stability when stored at 4 ℃ than room temperature. Meanwhile, the photostability and ion resistance of Cu NCs@TA were also displayed in Fig. S4 and Fig. S5, respectively. No significant weakening for the fluorescence of Cu NCs@TA were observed even after 10 min UV irradiation and 0.25 M NaCl solution.
Based on the above characterization results, the Cu NCs@TA was successfully fabricated through the above scheme with excellent stability.

The detection conditions played important roles in influencing test effect. To achieve the optimal detection conditions, factors of pH, reaction time, reaction temperature and ionic strength were used to study their response for the detection of CTC.

In the field of fluorescence detection, pH is a very important factor. The sensitivity of Cu NCs@TA for CTC sensing was investigated in conditions with different pH. As shown in Fig. 2A, the FL intensity of Cu NCs@TA could be kept above 91% varying from 6.0 to 8.0 (pH). The small influence of the pH value was very beneficial to the probe platform. And the FL intensity of Cu NCs@TA with CTC possessed similar change patterns under different pH values. From the above experimental results, 6.0 was selected as the ideal pH value.

The influence of reaction time on the fluorescence change of Cu NCs@TA with CTC was illustrated in Fig. 2B. The FL intensity of Cu NCs@TA maintained very stable, while the FL intensity of Cu NCs@TA with CTC gradually decreased. The further enlargement of the reaction time did not bring about any fall in the intensity, suggesting that the interaction between Cu NCs@TA and CTC reached equilibrium soon after two minutes. So, 2 min was selected as the optimal response time for the further experiments.

The effect of reaction temperature on the fluorescence response of Cu NCs@TA for CTC was also studied. As displayed in Fig. 2 C, with increasing the reaction temperature, the FL intensity of Cu NCs@TA gradually decreased, while the FL intensity of Cu NCs@TA+CTC remained basically unchanged. As the results，fluorescence change difference (F0-F) gradually decreased and 25 ℃ was selected as the optimal reaction temperature.

The sensitivity tests of Cu NCs@TA towards CTC were also investigated in NaCl solutions. As suggested in Fig. 2D, the FL intensity of Cu NCs@TA and Cu NCs@TA+CTC did not significantly decrease, even the NaCl concentration reached 0.25 M. It suggested that the ionic strength had negligible influence on the FL intensities of Cu NCs@TA and Cu NCs@TA+CTC.

The selectivity is an important index for fluorescent probes when used in actual applications. To discuss the selectivity of the Cu NCs@TA for measuring CTC, various interferes were separately added into the same batch of Cu NCs@TA solution, such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cl-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, Gly, Cys, Pro, Glu, Ala, AMP, PEN, MY, STR, CHL, GSH and CN. Firstly, the FL spectra of the Cu NCs@TA and PBS solution (pH=6.0) in the presence of interferes (200 μM) were recorded. It was discovered that an explosive fluorescence quenching appeared in the presence of CTC, while the FL intensities of other controls mainly unchanged (even when their concentration was 50 times higher than CTC) ( Fig. 3A). Additionally, the fluorescent photographs of Cu NCs@TA solution with CTC and other controls were demonstrated in Fig. 3B. It could be seen that the Cu NCs@TA+CTC was colorless, while the other solutions still showed blue fluorescence under UV lamp. Thus, a water-soluble Cu NCs@TA-based probe could be devised for highly sensitive determination of CTC.

The sensitivity of the Cu NCs@TA for CTC was discussed under the best sensing conditions. When different amounts of CTC were dropped into the Cu NCs@TA solution, an obvious decrease in the emission intensity around 435 nm was found and the corresponding spectra were also obtained ( Fig. 4). The linear relationship between ln(F0/F) and the concentration of CTC was inserted into Fig. 4. The linear range was 0.5–200 μM and the corresponding linear equation was ln(F0/F)= 0.017[CTC]+ 0.089, and the correlation coefficient could reach 0.9943. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was gauged to be 0.084 μM based on the 3σ/k (σ refers to the standard deviation and k stands for the slope of linear curve). The linear range was wider and the LOD was lower than the reported detection methods [9,10,29–32]. The mutual comparison of the above methods was listed in Table 1. Furthermore, when compared with other fluorescence nanomaterials, the synthetic strategy of Cu NCs@TA was rapid, facile and eco-friendly in aqueous solution.

Some research work was carried out to illustrate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the Cu NCs@TA by CTC. As displayed in Fig. S6(A), the UV-Vis spectrum of CTC significantly overlapped the fluorescence excitation spectrum of Cu NCs@TA, indicating that the fluorescence detection mechanism might be assumed to IFE or FRET [67,68]. As far as we know, IFE is due to the constant of fluorescence lifetime between probe and probe+detector, while FRET is the opposite [69]. The fluorescence lifetime of Cu NCs@TA and Cu NCs@TA+CTC were studied and its spectra were displayed in Fig. S6(B). The average fluorescence lifetime was calculated through the formula (τ = (A1τ1 2 +A2τ2 2)/(A1τ1 +A2τ2)) [70]. The average ﬂuorescence lifetimes of Cu NCs@TA and Cu NCs@TA+CTC were 1.733 and 1.721 ns based on the above formula. The not significant change in fluorescence lifetime indicated that the mechanism was attributed to IFE instead of FRET [71].

To confirm the IFE mechanism of CTC for Cu NCs@TA, the Parker equation was applied for the further experiments [72–74].
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 −
10
− d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 −
10
− s
A
em

Where Fobsd stands for the measured FL intensity, Fcor is the corrected FL intensity after considering IFE. Aex and Aem were the absorbance at 365 and 435 nm (Fig. S6(C)). The value of g is 0.40 cm, which refers to the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and edge of cuvette. s refers to the thickness of the excitation beam (0.10 cm). d represents the optical path length of light in solution (1.0 cm). The related parameters were calculated and displayed in Table 2. As shown in Table 2 and Fig. S6(D), with increasing the CTC concentrations, the Fcor/Fobsd value and the suppressed efficiency of observed (Eobsd) increased, and the Eobsd value was greater than the Ecor value. At the same time, a good linear relationship between the absorbance (365 nm) and the CTC concentration was observed. The above experimental results suggested that the quenching mechanism was IFE [75].

In addition, the main fluorescent quenching mechanisms include static and dynamic quenching. Fluorescence lifetime has not changed much between Cu NCs@TA and Cu NCs@TA+CTC. To further verify our conjecture, we also investigated fluorescence quenching mechanisms by using the Stern-Volmer equation.
F0/F=1+K SV[Q]=1+K qτ0[Q]

where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the Cu NCs@TA and the Cu NC@TA-CTC system, respectively. KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. [Q] represents the amount of CTC, and τ0 is the lifetime of Cu NCs@TA (1.733 ns). As shown in Fig. S7, KSV and Kq were calculated to be 1.835 × 104 L·mol−1 and 1.059 × 1013 L·mo1−1·s−1, respectively. The Kq value was greater than the maximum scatter collision quenching constant (2 ×1010 L·mol−1·s−1), indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to static quenching [76]. Therefore, the potential fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cu NCs@TA was devoted to the inner filter effect and static quenching.

To consider the possible application of Cu NCs@TA in real samples, the CTC amounts in bovine serum samples were measured. The spiked amounts of CTC included 20, 40 and 60 μM. The detection concentrations of CTC were calculated by using the linear equation. The satisfactory recoveries were obtained in the range 94.4%− 103.1% ( Table 3) and the RSD was low. The experimental data strongly verified that the sensor based on Cu NCs@TA for CTC sensing was an accurate method.

In summary, a fascinating fluorescent probe was successfully performed for the sensitive and selective sensing of CTC based on the TA capped Cu NCs. The facile one-pot preparation procedure and the friendly fluorescent probes made the Cu NCs@TA to be interesting. CTC-induced fluorescence quenching of Cu NCs@TA was assumed to the inner filter effect and static quenching mechanisms. When compared with other fluorescence nanomaterials, the synthetic strategy of Cu NCs@TA was rapid and facile with wide linear range and low LOD. Moreover, CTC was successfully detected in real samples with satisfactory recoveries. According to these amazing characteristics, we firmly believe that this paper developed new ideas for the program and application of Cu NCs in the field of medicine detection.

Yu-yu Guo: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Methodology. Yu-rui Hu: Investigation. Shao-ke Chen: Investigation. Ming-zhu Guo: Software. Yi-ting Zhang: Software. Xin-rui Han: Data analysis. Shen Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Project for College Students in Shanxi Province (No. 2020486) and the Fund for Shanxi “1331 Project” Collaborative Innovation Center (No. I018038) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127962.

ce:section.ce:para:
